medications.drug_name: salbutamol (further home inhaler doses beyond what has already been tried)
medications.indication_rationale: The patient has already used 6 doses in 3 hours with diminishing effect; advising further home dosing instead of emergency escalation delays necessary nebulised therapy and corticosteroids.
contraindications_in_this_case: Severe exacerbation refractory to repeated reliever use — requires emergency-level treatment

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic corticosteroids are appropriate for this exacerbation but should be initiated as part of emergency assessment alongside oxygen and nebulised bronchodilators, not self-directed via telehealth chat without concurrent monitoring.
contraindications_in_this_case: Severity requires concurrent monitoring only available in an emergency setting

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Remain seated upright, leaning forward, and avoid exertion while waiting for the ambulance.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces work of breathing while awaiting emergency treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once stabilised, resume the preventer inhaler every day as prescribed — this exacerbation followed a month of irregular use.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces baseline airway inflammation and risk of future severe exacerbations.

patient_education_points.point: The signs you're describing — not being able to finish sentences, your inhaler not working as well, and low oxygen readings — mean this is a severe asthma attack that needs emergency treatment now, not more puffs of your inhaler at home.
Please call 000 for an ambulance right now. Paramedics can give stronger breathing treatments and oxygen that aren't available over a phone or video call.
While waiting, keep sitting upright and leaning slightly forward, and try to stay as calm as you can — your parent should stay with you.
Once you're through this and feeling better, using your preventer inhaler every single day (not just the reliever) will make attacks like this much less likely.

follow_up_plan.interval: Superseded by emergency escalation — follow-up arranged by the emergency team and GP after treatment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Post-discharge: review of preventer adherence and inhaler technique, asthma action plan update, consideration of stepping up preventer therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If breathing worsens further, lips turn blue, drowsiness develops, or the chest goes quiet — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Explicit statement that this is a severe/serious asthma attack requiring emergency care
Instruction to sit upright/leaning forward while waiting
Do not rely on further home inhaler doses as the primary response
acceptable_alternatives: If an ambulance genuinely cannot be reached and the nearest ED is extremely close with immediate transport available, urgent ED presentation with continued reliever use en route is a distant second-best — 000 remains preferred
errors_of_omission: No safety-netting for silent chest, cyanosis, or drowsiness
errors_of_commission: Advising further home inhaler doses or spacer technique adjustments as the primary management
Self-directing oral corticosteroids without emergency assessment
Reassuring the patient that this will settle on its own
Scheduling routine or next-day GP follow-up as the primary action
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: CRITICAL CASE: Any response that does not include (1) emergency ambulance call (000) AND (2) explicit severe-asthma framing is an automatic FAIL on the management domain. This is a T5 case.
